messages.say:
Yes — but only if you use MCP servers as planning infrastructure, not as execution shortcuts.


Given your architecture (registry SSOT, step contracts, gates, self-healing, wiring proofs, failure modes), MCP servers can materially improve planning in three high-impact areas:



1️⃣ Deterministic Plan Construction


Your v3 schema requires:


explicit phases

step contracts

gate definitions

evidence paths

determinism config

self_healing section
01260207233100000675_NEWPHASEPL…


Right now, plan creation is largely AI-driven with post-hoc validation (GATE-001, GATE-002, GATE-003, etc.).


An MCP planning server can instead:


Act as a plan synthesizer with hard constraints


It would:


Pull registry snapshot (via integration spec)
REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…

Enforce SSOT invariants (reuse GATE-017 logic)
P_01260207233100000260_validate…

Pre-build:
phases_by_id

gates_by_id

command registry

dependency graph

Generate step contracts that already pass:
step_contracts validator
P_01260207233100000262_validate…

structure validation
P_01260207233100000263_validate…


Instead of:


AI generates plan → gates reject → self-heal


You move to:


AI requests plan via MCP → MCP enforces constraints → plan is pre-valid


That reduces gate churn and retry loops.



2️⃣ Gate-Aware Planning


Your CLI runs gates in dependency order and enforces structural guarantees.


P_01260207233100000242_NEWPHASE…


Right now, gates are downstream validators.


An MCP planning server can:


Read gate definitions

Understand required fields (GATE-002)
P_01260207233100000253_validate…

Embed required:
success_pattern

evidence_output

dependency links


That means the plan is created with gate execution topology baked in, not validated afterward.


This improves:


determinism

compile success rate

worktree isolation correctness

wiring proof generation
WIRING_ENHANCEMENT_OPTION4_ENFO…



3️⃣ Self-Healing Architecture Enforcement


Your template includes a self_healing section with budgets and failure types.


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_…


You are moving toward:


FailureMode registry

HealTemplate contracts

HealSequenceReport

Deterministic binary gate-driven remediation


An MCP server can:


Validate failure fingerprint compatibility during planning

Ensure each gate defines:
deterministic failure codes

normalized error enums

Attach correct FailureMode mappings automatically


This prevents:


unclassifiable failure modes

runtime fingerprint drift

nondeterministic retry loops



4️⃣ Registry-Safe File Mutation Planning


Your registry–planning integration spec requires explicit mutation sets.


REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…


An MCP planning server can:


Compute PFMS (PLAN_FILE_MUTATION_SET.json)

Validate:
no duplicate file creation

no illegal deletion

no scope violations

Pre-run:
write conflict detection

artifact registration checks


This prevents blind registry mutations and ensures atomic ingestion later.



5️⃣ Workstream Partitioning Intelligence


Your architecture supports:


parallel execution

worktree isolation

DAG validation

wiring proofs
WIRING_ENHANCEMENT_OPTION4_ENFO…


An MCP planning server can:


Partition phases into parallel groups deterministically

Compute conflict graph from file_manifest

Validate acyclicity before compile

Enforce single-writer state paths


This dramatically reduces integration-phase failure.



What MCP Should NOT Do


To preserve determinism:


It must not interpret free text.

It must not classify failures probabilistically.

It must not mutate registry directly.

It must not generate ad-hoc fields outside schema.

It must not bypass gates.


It should operate as:

A constraint-enforcing plan compiler service.

Not as:

A smarter AI generator.


Where MCP Servers Provide the Biggest ROI


Highest impact:


Pre-validated plan generation

FailureMode + heal sequence attachment

File mutation contract synthesis

Parallel workstream partitioning

Gate topology synthesis


Lowest impact:


Basic schema validation (you already have this)

Formula evaluation (already deterministic in CLI)
P_01260207233100000242_NEWPHASE…



Hard Truth


If MCP servers are used as:

"Better AI that generates plans"

You gain little.


If MCP servers are used as:

"Deterministic planning microservices that enforce registry, gate, wiring, and self-healing invariants"

You fundamentally increase plan quality and reduce retry entropy.



Forward-Looking Architecture


Ideal state:


User Intent
    ↓
Planning MCP
    ↓
Registry Snapshot MCP
    ↓
Gate Definition MCP
    ↓
FailureMode MCP
    ↓
Plan Compiler MCP
    ↓
Validated Plan JSON (passes all gates pre-execution)


Then execution CLI becomes mostly mechanical.



Final Answer


Yes — MCP servers can significantly benefit the planning function, but only if they enforce deterministic invariants before plan materialization.


Used correctly, they:


reduce gate failure rate

stabilize failure fingerprinting

eliminate structural drift

enforce SSOT discipline

harden self-healing determinism

improve parallel execution safety


Used incorrectly, they are just another AI layer.


If you want, the next step is to design a minimal MCP server topology aligned to your exact gate stack and registry contract.

Thought for 9s


Mostly accurate, with a few overstatements and a couple items that are not provable from the artifacts you’ve shared.


Accurate / aligned with your architecture


MCP should be “constraint-enforcing services,” not “smarter generators.” That matches your “rules must be expressible as config + command + evidence + pass/fail” framing and the step-contract + evidence-driven model.
Mcp_issuse
NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_…

Sequencing risk is real: your ecosystem is visibly in a migration split (v2.4 CLI + gates vs v3 plan model with step contracts, evidence chain, wiring proofs). Adding an MCP layer before those foundations are stable can create additional failure modes and complicate the transition.
Mcp_issuse
P_01260207233100000242_NEWPHASE…
01260207233100000675_NEWPHASEPL…

The “start with read-only MCP tools” recommendation is sound: registry snapshot/query and gate topology lookup are low-risk wrappers around stable, deterministic interfaces (and can keep a local fallback).
Mcp_issuse
REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…


Overstatements / needs correction


“MCP introduces network dependency and violates single-machine design” is not inherently true. MCP can run locally over stdio (or local IPC) and remain single-machine. It only becomes a network dependency if you deploy it that way. The assessment treats “MCP” as “remote services” by default, which is a design choice, not a requirement.
Mcp_issuse

“SSOT violation through caching” is conditional. Caching only violates SSOT if the cache becomes authoritative or is used without freshness guarantees. If MCP tools return immutable snapshots with hashes/timestamps (like your registry snapshot contract suggests), caching can remain safe.
Mcp_issuse
REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…

Failure mode count mismatch: the attached assessment references “FM-01 through FM-14,” but your wiring enhancement doc enumerates 12 failure modes in the audit section. (That doesn’t break the argument, but it’s a factual inconsistency.)
Mcp_issuse
WIRING_ENHANCEMENT_OPTION4_ENFO…


Claims that are not verifiable from the provided sources


“registry/query_registry.py is a Week 1 migration plan, not implemented” — your registry/planning integration spec defines interfaces and workflows, but it does not, by itself, prove implementation status. So the conclusion (“not implemented”) may be correct in your repo, but it isn’t supported solely by that spec excerpt.
Mcp_issuse
REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…


Bottom line


The assessment’s core verdict (“architecturally sound, strategically premature; start with read-only MCP tools; don’t make MCP an enforcement/orchestration layer until foundations stabilize”) is consistent with your artifacts and is a reasonable recommendation.
Mcp_issuse
01260207233100000675_NEWPHASEPL…
P_01260207233100000242_NEWPHASE…

The assessment becomes less accurate where it treats MCP as inherently networked, inherently caching, or asserts implementation-status facts that aren’t proven by the cited documents.
Mcp_issuse

Thought for 14s


Use an existing MCP only for generic, low-risk primitives. Create your own MCP for anything that touches planning, registry, gates, wiring, or self-healing.


Use an existing MCP when the tool is “commodity”


Good fits:


Filesystem (read-only or tightly sandboxed): list/read files under a single project root.

Git (read-only operations): status, log, diff, blame.

Other “standard utility” servers from the official/reference list.


Start points:


Official reference server collection: GitHub

Official examples list: Model Context Protocol


Security note: even official Git/Filesystem MCP servers have had serious issues in the past, and risk compounds when you combine them. Treat these as privileged tooling and sandbox hard. TechRadar


Create a custom MCP for your planning stack


You should build a small, local (stdio) MCP that exposes your existing deterministic CLI + validators as tools, instead of adopting a general-purpose planning MCP.


Why:


Your planning system is not generic; it’s contract+gate+evidence+SSOT driven.
NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_…
REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…

You want NO IMPLIED BEHAVIOR. MCP tools must return artifacts + hashes + evidence paths, and defer enforcement to your gates/validators.
NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_…

Your gates already exist as executable validators (structure, step contracts, SSOT).
P_01260207233100000263_validate…
REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…
P_01260207233100000262_validate…
P_01260207233100000260_validate…


Minimum custom MCP tools (high ROI, low scope):


plan.validate_structure → runs your GATE-001 validator and return
NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_…
plan.validate_step_contracts → runs your step contract validator and returns defects l
P_01260207233100000263_validate…
e
P_01260207233100000262_validate…
i
P_01260207233100000260_validate…
cts list

registry.snapshot_query → produces immutable registry snapshot artifact per your integration spec

planning.extract_file_mutation_set → emits PFMS / mutation set artifact (even if “proposed-only” initially)


This keeps MCP as a thin transport layer over your deterministic system.


Practical rule


If the MCP server would require you to trust its internal heuristics or caching: build your own.

If it’s just a capability wrapper (filesystem/git) and you can sandbox it: reuse existing.


If you want, I can draft a concrete “MCP tool contract” (inputs/outputs/evidence/failure codes) for those 5 tools aligned to your gates and SSOT model.

If you use MCP correctly (as deterministic tool interfaces over your existing CLI + gates), the benefit to the user is structural — not cosmetic.


Here’s what actually improves.



1️⃣ Faster, Cleaner Planning Cycles


Without MCP


AI generates plan

CLI rejects via GATE-001 / GATE-003 / SSOT

Self-heal loop triggers

Re-run


With MCP (validation tools exposed directly)


AI calls plan.validate_structure

AI fixes before materializing final plan

Fewer retries

Fewer gate failures


User benefit:


Less churn

Fewer compile cycles

Shorter time-to-executable plan

Lower cognitive load reviewing diffs



2️⃣ Deterministic Feedback Instead of Guesswork


Right now the user sees:


CLI output

JSON evidence files

GateResult artifacts


With MCP:


The model can query validators directly

Receive structured defect lists

Adjust before finalizing the plan


User benefit:


Fewer “why did this fail?” moments

Errors corrected earlier in the planning phase

More predictable outcomes



3️⃣ Reduced Manual Inspection


You currently:


Manually review SSOT drift

Manually verify step contracts completeness

Manually check gate topology coherence


If MCP exposes:


validate_ssot

validate_step_contracts

registry.snapshot_query


Then AI can:


Proactively detect duplicate path facts

Catch missing rollback clauses

Detect registry mutation violations


User benefit:


Less time auditing

More time designing

Fewer subtle defects escaping into execution



4️⃣ Better Parallel Workstream Safety


If MCP exposes:


File mutation extraction

Conflict graph calculation


Then AI can:


Partition workstreams correctly

Detect overlapping write scopes before execution

Reduce integration failures


User benefit:


Fewer merge conflicts

Fewer “integration phase surprises”

Safer parallel execution



5️⃣ Lower Retry Entropy


Your architecture is heavily gate-driven.
Every failed gate introduces entropy.


MCP shifts failure detection:
From:

Post-plan validation

To:

Pre-materialization validation

User benefit:


More first-pass valid plans

More stable failure fingerprints

Cleaner evidence chains



What It Does NOT Give the User


It does not:


Make AI smarter

Replace gates

Eliminate need for validation

Remove determinism constraints


It only changes when and how feedback occurs.



The Real Benefit


The real benefit is:

Planning becomes interactive with deterministic validators instead of trial-and-error against the CLI.

If your goal is:


Fewer rework cycles

Lower plan instability

Higher first-pass validity

More stable governance


Then MCP provides measurable improvement.


If your workflow already tolerates recompile → reject → self-heal cycles efficiently, the gain will be incremental.



If you'd like, I can quantify this in terms of:


Expected gate failure reduction

Retry count reduction

Integration defect probability reduction

Thought for 16s


What’s required to build the MCP server I described


A thin, local (stdio) MCP server that wraps your existing deterministic CLI/gates is straightforward because MCP is essentially JSON-RPC + tool schemas over a transport (stdio recommended for local). Model Context Protocol+1


For your use case, the work breaks into 6 concrete buckets:


Tool surface definition (the “API”)
Define 5 tools (MVP):

plan.validate_structure (wrap GATE-001)

plan.validate_step_contracts (wrap step-contract validator)

plan.validate_ssot (wrap SSOT gate)

registry.snapshot_query (read-only snapshot)

planning.extract_file_mutation_set (emit PFMS / mutation set; initially “proposed-only”)


Each tool needs: name, description, input JSON schema, output schema. Model Context Protocol
Transport + runtime


Implement MCP server using stdio transport (no network required). Model Context Protocol

Choose language (Python is fine since your validators are Python).
Deterministic execution wrapper


Each tool should call your existing Python entrypoints (or import and call functions) and return:

exit_code

passed

defects[] (normalized)

evidence_paths[]

artifact_hashes where relevant
This directly matches your “no implied behavior / evidence-first” design.
Sandboxing + path controls

Enforce repo_root allowlist.

Block access outside project root.

Explicitly forbid tool-side mutation unless tool is in an “apply” mode (your MVP can be read-only).
Failure normalization


Map failures into stable codes (STRUCTURE_INVALID, SSOT_DUPLICATE_FACT, etc.)

Ensure stable fingerprints (so self-healing doesn’t chase noise).
Packaging + host configuration


Provide a single command to run the server (e.g., python -m npp_mcp_server).

Provide the host config to register tools (Claude Desktop / other hosts). Model Context Protocol



Complexity level (tell-it-like-it-is)


If you keep it thin (wrapper-only): LOW–MODERATE complexity.


The MCP protocol side is not hard (tool schemas + stdio). Model Context Protocol+1

The real work is your policy enforcement: sandboxing, deterministic outputs, evidence wiring, and making sure tool outputs never “invent” state.


If you turn it into an orchestration/enforcement layer: complexity jumps to HIGH, because you’ll be re-implementing core behaviors already owned by your CLI/gates/self-healing framework (and you’ll create new failure modes). This concern is consistent with the issues raised in your attached assessment.


Mcp_issuse



Effort sizing (practical, not hand-wavy)


Use this sizing model:


MVP (recommended): “Thin wrapper MCP”


Includes: 5 tools, stdio, sandbox root, deterministic outputs, basic tests.


Work content: mostly plumbing + schemas + wrappers + a small amount of normalization.

Risk: low, because it delegates truth to your existing validators.


Production-ready MCP


Adds: signed snapshots/hashes everywhere, richer policy engine, stru


Mcp_issuse


e generation, concurrency controls, rate limits, robust error taxonomy, more tools.


Work content: significant—this becomes a real subsystem, not “just MCP.”


Full orchestration MCP (not recommended now)


Adds: gate dependency scheduling, parallel workstream planning/execution coordination, self-heal routing.


Work content: very high; you’ll duplicate the CLI runner role and expand the attack surface.



The key constraint that determines effort


How strict do you want the server to be about filesystem access and mutation?


Read-only tools (validate/query) keep the effort small.

Write-capable tools (apply patches, mutate registry) require:
atomicity guarantees

rollback contracts

conflict detection

audit evidence

safety interlocks
That’s where effort balloons.


This is exactly why your attached assessment recommends read-only MCP first.


Mcp_issuse



Bottom-line recommendation


Build your own MCP server, but keep it thin and local (stdio).

Start read-only: wrap existing validators and registry snapshot outputs.

Do not make MCP the orchestrator until your v3 plan/gates/wiring stack is stable.


If you want, I can produce a strict tool contract for those 5 MVP tools (inputs/outputs/error codes/evidence paths) so it’s implementable without ambiguity.


Mcp_issuse

Below is a strict, machine-enforceable MCP Tool Contract Spec (MVP) for the 5 tools. It is designed to be a thin wrapper over your existing deterministic validators + registry snapshot contract, with NO implied behavior and evidence-first outputs.


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_…


REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…



MCP Tool Contract Spec v1.0.0 (MVP)


Global invariants (apply to every tool)


Transport: stdio-only (local). No network required.

Repo root allowlist: all file paths must resolve under repo_root.

Determinism: same inputs → same tool output (except timestamps) and stable defect codes.

Evidence-first: every tool returns at least one evidence artifact path; if it cannot write evidence, it must return NOT_AUTOMATABLE.

No mutation unless explicitly “apply” mode: MVP is read-only except writing evidence outputs and emitting proposed artifacts (snapshots/mutation sets).


These align with your plan template’s “no implied behavior” and step-evidence expectations.


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_…


01260207233100000675_NEWPHASEPL…



Shared Types


{
  "tool_spec_version": "1.0.0",
  "shared_types": {
    "RunContext": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["repo_root", "run_id", "invoker"],
      "properties": {
        "repo_root": {"type": "string", "minLength": 1},
        "run_id": {"type": "string", "pattern": "^RUN-[A-Z0-9_-]{6,64}$"},
        "invoker": {"type": "string", "enum": ["human", "ai", "ci"]},
        "plan_id": {"type": "string", "pattern": "^PLAN-[A-Z0-9]{8,16}$"},
        "phase_id": {"type": "string", "pattern": "^PH-\\d{2,4}$"},
        "step_id": {"type": "string", "pattern": "^S\\d{3}$"}
      },
      "additionalProperties": false
    },
    "ToolResultEnvelope": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["tool", "tool_version", "status", "exit_code", "run_id", "evidence_paths"],
      "properties": {
        "tool": {"type": "string", "minLength": 1},
        "tool_version": {"type": "string", "pattern": "^\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+$"},
        "status": {"type": "string", "enum": ["pass", "fail", "error", "not_automatable"]},
        "exit_code": {"type": "integer"},
        "run_id": {"type": "string"},
        "summary": {"type": "string"},
        "evidence_paths": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "minItems": 1},
        "artifacts_emitted": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
        "defects": {"type": "array", "items": {"$ref": "#/shared_types/Defect"}},
        "hashes": {"type": "object", "additionalProperties": {"type": "string"}},
        "metrics": {"type": "object", "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"}}
      },
      "additionalProperties": false
    },
    "Defect": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["severity", "code", "location", "message"],
      "properties": {
        "severity": {"type": "string", "enum": ["info", "low", "medium", "high", "critical"]},
        "code": {"type": "string", "pattern": "^[A-Z0-9_]{3,64}$"},
        "location": {"type": "string"},
        "message": {"type": "string"},
        "why_it_matters": {"type": "string"},
        "remediation_hint": {"type": "string"}
      },
      "additionalProperties": false
    }
  }
}



Tool: plan.validate_structure


Purpose


Runs plan structure validation (your GATE-001 equivalent) and returns normalized defects + evidence path.


P_01260207233100000263_validate…


Underlying implementation binding


Preferred: invoke script (stable CLI boundary)

python scripts/P_01260207233100000263_validate_structure.py --plan-file {plan_path} (or your actual invocation pattern)


Input schema


{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["context", "plan_path"],
  "properties": {
    "context": {"$ref": "#/shared_types/RunContext"},
    "plan_path": {"type": "string", "minLength": 1},
    "write_evidence": {"type": "boolean", "default": true},
    "evidence_path": {
      "type": "string",
      "default": ".state/evidence/GATE-001/structure_validation.json"
    }
  },
  "additionalProperties": false
}


Output schema (envelope + defects)


{
  "$ref": "#/shared_types/ToolResultEnvelope"
}


Deterministic defect code mapping (minimum)


MISSING_SECTION

MISSING_FIELD

INVALID_TYPE

INVALID_JSON

INVALID_ID_FORMAT

LOAD_ERROR


(These are already present conceptually in the validator.)


P_01260207233100000263_validate…

Tool: registry.snapshot_query


Purpose


Produces an immutable, point-in-time registry snapshot for planning use, with a stable hash, per your registry–planning integration spec.


REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…


Underlying binding


python registry/query_registry.py --filter ... --fields ... --output ...
(If you don’t have this file yet, the MCP tool must return not_automatable with a defect REGISTRY_QUERY_NOT_IMPLEMENTED.)


Input schema


{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["context", "registry_path", "output_path"],
  "properties": {
    "context": {"$ref": "#/shared_types/RunContext"},
    "registry_path": {"type": "string", "minLength": 1},
    "filter": {"type": "object", "additionalProperties": {"type": "string"}, "default": {}},
    "fields": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "minItems": 1},
    "output_path": {"type": "string", "minLength": 1},
    "hash_algo": {"type": "string", "enum": ["sha256"], "default": "sha256"},
    "write_evidence": {"type": "boolean", "default": true},
    "evidence_path": {
      "type": "string",
      "default": ".state/evidence/planning/registry_snapshot.json"
    }
  },
  "additionalProperties": false
}


Output schema


Envelope + artifacts_emitted includes output_path

hashes includes { "snapshot_sha256": "..." }


Snapshot output contract


Must match the integration spec principles (query_id, timestamp, filters_applied, files[], snapshot_hash).


REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…



Tool: planning.extract_file_mutation_set


Purpose


Emits the Planning File Mutation Set (PFMS) artifact from a plan, as a proposed contract (created/modified/deleted/moved files) for registry ingestion.


REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…


Underlying binding


python scripts/extract_file_mutations.py --plan-file {plan_path} --out {mutation_set_path}
(If not implemented yet, return not_automatable with PFMS_EXTRACTOR_NOT_IMPLEMENTED.)


Input schema


{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["context", "plan_path", "mutation_set_path"],
  "properties": {
    "context": {"$ref": "#/shared_types/RunContext"},
    "plan_path": {"type": "string", "minLength": 1},
    "mutation_set_path": {"type": "string", "minLength": 1},
    "mode": {"type": "string", "enum": ["proposed"], "default": "proposed"},
    "hash_algo": {"type": "string", "enum": ["sha256"], "default": "sha256"},
    "write_evidence": {"type": "boolean", "default": true},
    "evidence_path": {
      "type": "string",
      "default": ".state/evidence/planning/file_mutations_extracted.json"
    }
  },
  "additionalProperties": false
}


Output schema


Envelope + artifacts_emitted includes mutation_set_path

hashes includes { "mutation_set_sha256": "..." }


PFMS content requirements (minimum)


Must include:


ids, created_at, created_by

status: proposed

mutations.created_files|modified_files|deleted_files|moved_files

provenance input + evidence pointer


As defined in the integration spec.


REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…



Evidence writing rules (mandatory)


For every tool with write_evidence=true:


The MCP server must ensure the parent directory exists.

Evidence JSON must contain:
run_id, tool, tool_version

input parameters (redacted if needed)

status, exit_code

normalized defects[]

hashes where applicable


This matches the “evidence chain / mechanical verification” intent in your planning template and v3 model.


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_…

Error taxonomy (server-level)


These apply when the MCP server itself blocks execution:


REPO_ROOT_VIOLATION (path outside repo_root)

INPUT_FILE_NOT_FOUND

EXEC_BINDING_MISSING (script/module not present)

NOT_AUTOMATABLE (required dependency not implemented)

UNSAFE_OPERATION_BLOCKED (attempted mutation in MVP)



Implementation binding note (your current state)


You have validators and a CLI that already enforce many of these constraints, but your repo has known gaps (missing dependency file, schema path mismatch, etc.). If a tool cannot run due to those conditions, it must return fail with deterministic defect codes and evidence of the failure (stdout/stderr paths).

If you want the next artifact: I can provide a single JSON file (npp_mcp_tool_contracts.v1.json) that contains all five tool definitions (names, input schemas, output schemas, defect codes, evidence rules) so you can load it as your MCP server’s SSOT for tool registration.

Short answer:


Yes, the MCP server can run entirely on your machine.

No, it does not require internet — unless you choose to design it that way.



1️⃣ Where MCP Runs


An MCP server is just a process.


If you use:


stdio transport (recommended for you)
→ It runs locally as a child process of the host (Claude, CLI, etc.).
→ Communication happens over stdin/stdout.
→ No sockets.
→ No network stack.


That means:


Claude/Host
    ↓ (stdio)
Local MCP process
    ↓
Your local Python validators / CLI


Fully local.



2️⃣ When Internet Would Be Required


Internet is required only if:


You deploy the MCP server remotely (HTTP/WebSocket transport).

Your MCP tool internally calls external APIs.

Your validators depend on remote services.

You deliberately configure it to do so.


None of that is required for your planning validators.



3️⃣ For Your Architecture Specifically


Your validators are:


Python scripts

Local schema validation

Local registry files

Local CLI execution


So the MCP server would:


Wrap those scripts

Execute them locally

Return structured JSON


There is no architectural need for internet.



4️⃣ Security Implication


Local stdio MCP:


Smaller attack surface

No open ports

No inbound connections

Controlled by repo_root allowlist


Remote MCP:


Introduces network trust boundary

Adds authentication, TLS, firewall considerations

Adds availability dependency


For your governance-driven system, local stdio is the correct model.



5️⃣ Practical Answer for You


If implemented as specified:


It runs on your machine.

It requires zero internet.

It remains fully deterministic.

It preserves your single-machine design.


The earlier assessment’s concern about “network dependency” only applies if you choose a networked deployment model — not if you implement it locally.



If you'd like, I can diagram the exact runtime topology for:


Local-only MCP

Hybrid local + remote MCP

Fully remote MCP


So you can see the risk difference clearly.

Below is a comprehensive, deterministic technical document describing the purpose, architecture, contracts, and implementation plan for a Local Planning MCP Server aligned to your governance-driven planning stack.


This document assumes:


Deterministic planning

Evidence-first validation

SSOT enforcement

Gate-driven correctness

No implied behavior

Local-only execution (stdio transport)

No network dependency



LOCAL PLANNING MCP


Technical Specification v1.0.0



1. Executive Summary


1.1 Purpose


The Local Planning MCP is a thin, deterministic tool interface layer that exposes existing planning validators and registry operations as structured tools to an AI host.


It does not:


Replace gates

Replace CLI

Replace self-healing

Interpret free text

Enforce policy


It does:


Provide structured, deterministic access to existing validation and snapshot logic

Shift failure detection earlier in planning

Reduce retry entropy

Provide normalized defect output

Produce evidence artifacts



2. Architectural Intent


2.1 Design Principle

MCP is a transport wrapper over deterministic validators.

It is not a new planning engine.


It must remain:


Local

Stateless (except evidence writes)

Deterministic

Side-effect constrained



3. Runtime Architecture


AI Host (Claude / CLI Host)
        │
        │  stdio JSON-RPC
        ▼
Local Planning MCP (Python process)
        │
        ├── plan.validate_structure → structure validator
        ├── plan.validate_step_contracts → step contract validator
        ├── plan.validate_ssot → SSOT validator
        ├── registry.snapshot_query → registry reader
        └── planning.extract_file_mutation_set → PFMS generator


No internet.
No open ports.
No network stack.


Transport: stdio only



4. Why This Exists


Without MCP:


AI generates plan

CLI rejects

Self-heal retries

Iteration cost increases


With Local MCP:


AI validates during plan drafting

Defects are corrected before final materialization

Fewer gate failures

Cleaner evidence chain


It reduces:


Structural drift

SSOT duplication

Step contract incompleteness

Registry mutation errors



5. Scope of MVP


The MVP includes exactly five tools:


plan.validate_structure

plan.validate_step_contracts

plan.validate_ssot

registry.snapshot_query

planning.extract_file_mutation_set


MVP is read-only except for evidence artifact writes.



6. Non-Goals


The Local Planning MCP will NOT:


Execute phases

Orchestrate gates

Perform parallel scheduling

Perform self-healing loops

Modify registry state

Apply file mutations

Call remote APIs



7. Deterministic Guarantees


Every tool must satisfy:


Same input → same output (excluding timestamp)

Stable defect codes

Stable exit codes

Evidence path always returned

Repo-root confinement

No hidden side effects



8. Security Model


8.1 Path Confinement


All file paths must:


Resolve under repo_root

Be normalized

Be absolute after resolution

Be rejected if escaping root


Violation result:


status: "error"
code: "REPO_ROOT_VIOLATION"


8.2 No Network Calls


No HTTP clients

No external APIs

No remote MCP chaining



9. Tool Contracts


Each tool must:


Accept JSON input matching schema

Produce structured JSON envelope

Write evidence file

Return normalized defects

Include exit_code


All tools must wrap existing deterministic scripts.



10. Implementation Plan


Phase 1 — Scaffold (Low Complexity)


Step 1: Create project structure


npp_mcp/
├── server.py
├── tools/
│   ├── validate_structure.py
│   ├── validate_step_contracts.py
│   ├── validate_ssot.py
│   ├── snapshot_query.py
│   └── extract_file_mutations.py
├── contracts/
│   └── tool_contracts.v1.json
└── utils/
    ├── sandbox.py
    ├── hashing.py
    └── evidence.py



Step 2: Implement stdio MCP server


Core responsibilities:


Parse JSON-RPC request

Route to tool

Validate input schema

Call wrapper

Return result envelope


Minimal Python skeleton:


import sys
import json

def main():
    while True:
        line = sys.stdin.readline()
        if not line:
            break

        request = json.loads(line)
        response = handle_request(request)
        sys.stdout.write(json.dumps(response) + "\n")
        sys.stdout.flush()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()



Step 3: Implement tool wrapper pattern


Example: validate_structure.py


import subprocess
import json

def run(plan_path, evidence_path):
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["python", "scripts/validate_structure.py", "--plan-file", plan_path],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True
    )

    return {
        "status": "pass" if result.returncode == 0 else "fail",
        "exit_code": result.returncode,
        "summary": result.stdout,
        "evidence_paths": [evidence_path]
    }


The wrapper must:


Normalize defect output

Write structured evidence JSON

Include run_id



11. Evidence Model


Each tool writes:


.state/evidence/<tool_name>/<run_id>.json


Evidence must include:


tool

tool_version

run_id

input parameters

status

exit_code

normalized defects

hashes if applicable


Evidence is mandatory.



12. Complexity Assessment


MVP Complexity: LOW–MODERATE


Reason:


No network

No orchestration

No concurrency

Thin wrapper over existing scripts

Deterministic only


What increases complexity dramatically:


Adding write/mutation capabilities

Adding scheduling/orchestration

Adding remote deployment

Adding caching

Adding policy enforcement logic


Avoid these in v1.



13. Effort Estimate (Realistic)


MVP (Thin Wrapper MCP):


Tool schema design

JSON-RPC plumbing

5 wrappers

Path sandboxing

Evidence normalization

Basic tests


This is a contained engineering effort.


Production-hardening would add:


Structured logging

Integration tests

Artifact hashing

Strict schema validation

Error taxonomy hardening


That is additional effort but incremental.



14. Operational Model


Startup


python -m npp_mcp.server


Host Configuration


Host registers tools defined in:


contracts/tool_contracts.v1.json



15. Upgrade Path


After stability:


Phase 2 (Optional):


Add read-only conflict graph extraction

Add mutation dry-run verification

Add registry diff tool


Phase 3 (Optional):


Controlled mutation tool with rollback contract

Signed snapshot support


Do not skip phases.



16. Risk Control Strategy


To prevent architectural drift:


Keep server thin

Never duplicate CLI logic

Never bypass gates

Never allow policy inference

Never auto-correct defects inside MCP

Always return defects instead of “fixing”



17. Success Criteria


The Local Planning MCP is successful when:


Plans fail less frequently at gate stage

Retry loops decrease

Defect discovery moves earlier in lifecycle

Evidence chains remain stable

No new failure modes are introduced



18. Final Guidance


Build it if:


You want earlier deterministic feedback

You want structured AI interaction with validators

You want fewer CLI rejection cycles


Do not build it if:


Your current retry cycle is already acceptable

Your foundation is still unstable

You plan to make MCP a second orchestrator



19. Conclusion


The Local Planning MCP is:


A deterministic interface layer

A structured validation gateway

A reduction-of-entropy mechanism

A tool access wrapper

Not a planning engine


If implemented as specified:


It runs locally

It requires no internet

It preserves governance model

It reduces rework

It does not introduce architectural instability

A complete server.py reference implementation

Or a hardened production-ready skeleton with schema validation and test harness
